question: How does the 12-module plant concept in Ch.01 relate to the single-module DHRS analysis in Ch.05?

nuscale_ch01_0006.title: Power Output
nuscale_ch01_0006.summary: The NuScale Power Plant consists of up to 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), each producing 160 MWt (thermal) and approximately 50 MWe (electric), for a total net output of about 570 MWe when accounting for house loads. The actual electrical output may vary depending on environmental conditions, and the design includes a 2% margin for measurement uncertainty.

nuscale_ch01_0128.title: 1.9.3 Generic Issues
nuscale_ch01_0128.summary: This section describes NuScale’s approach to ensuring conformance with regulatory requirements, including the Standard Review Plan (SRP), Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS), and NRC Interim Staff Guidance. It outlines how NuScale addresses technically relevant Three Mile Island (TMI) requirements and unresolved or high-priority generic safety issues, referencing applicable regulations and NRC guidance documents.

nuscale_ch01_0127.title: 1.9.2 Conformance with Standard Review Plan
nuscale_ch01_0127.summary: NuScale reviewed the Standard Review Plan (SRP), including all relevant guidance and acceptance criteria, and documented the results in a gap analysis report submitted to the NRC. The company also assessed conformance with the Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) and NRC Interim Staff Guidance, with details provided in referenced tables. Additionally, NuScale addressed compliance with applicable Three Mile Island (TMI) requirements and identified resolutions for any relevant unresolved or generic safety issues as required by NRC regulations.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components within the NuScale reactor coolant system, specifying alloys and standards for each part to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility. It references relevant sections of the BPVC (Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code) and lists the applicable material specifications for components such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat following reactor shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring the reactor coolant system (RCS) remains within safe temperature and pressure limits. It operates without external power, maintains the RCS average temperature below 420°F within 36 hours, and its reliability and risk significance are rigorously evaluated. The DHRS does not require Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) activation but can function alongside ECCS if needed.

nuscale_ch01_0007.title: Schedule
nuscale_ch01_0007.summary: The section describes the NuScale containment vessel as a cylindrical, ASME-certified steel structure designed to withstand high-pressure events, with internal vacuum conditions during normal operation. The NuScale reactor is a passive, integral small modular pressurized water reactor, with each module producing about 50 MWe, and a 12-module plant yielding a net output of approximately 570 MWe. The schedule requirement specifies that applicants referencing the NuScale design must provide their own construction and implementation schedules.

nuscale_ch01_0315.title: SRP 9.2.1, Rev 5: Station Service Water SystemII.1 Protection Against Natural Phenomena (GDC 2)
nuscale_ch01_0315.summary: The NuScale Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) is not safety-related or risk-significant and does not provide essential cooling to safety-related structures, systems, and components (SSC). Regulatory criteria such as GDC 2, GDC 4, and GDC 5 are only applicable to ensure that failure of the nonsafety-related SCWS does not adversely affect safety-related SSCs, and the system’s design and layout meet these limited requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0004.title: Containment Type
nuscale_ch01_0004.summary: The NuScale containment vessel is a cylindrical, steel, ASME-certified pressure vessel designed to withstand high-pressure events and operates under vacuum during normal conditions, eliminating the need for insulation. The NuScale reactor is a passive, integral small modular pressurized water reactor (SMR) with natural circulation and no external piping for steam generators or pressurizer, and each module produces about 50 MWe, with a 12-module plant yielding a net output of approximately 570 MWe.

nuscale_ch01_0003.title: Plant Location
nuscale_ch01_0003.summary: The section introduces the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), which is required for standard design certification under 10 CFR 52. It describes the modular and scalable NuScale Power Module (NPM), highlighting its key safety features, compact design, and the ability to operate between one and twelve modules within a single facility. The FSAR covers both individual module design and multi-module plant interfaces, emphasizing safety, efficiency, and advanced digital controls.

nuscale_ch01_0060.title: NuScale Integral System Test Program
nuscale_ch01_0060.summary: The NuScale Integral System Test Program was established to generate thermal-hydraulic data for system characterization and to validate safety analysis codes using a scaled model of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design. The NIST-1 facility replicates key reactor systems and provides essential data for validating proprietary safety codes, supporting both safety methodology development and the Design Certification Application.

nuscale_ch01_0277.title: DSRS 8.4, Rev 0: Station BlackoutII.3 Independence of SBO-related power sources
nuscale_ch01_0277.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for station blackout (SBO) and related power systems. It notes that while NuScale partially conforms to several regulatory guides regarding independence and requirements of SBO-related power sources, the design does not rely on AC power for 72 hours post-SBO, and the independence of DC power supplies (EDSS) is addressed in referenced sections. Some requirements, such as those related to maintenance rules and alternate AC power sources, are either not applicable or the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0035.title: Fuel Handling and Reactor Maintenance Areas
nuscale_ch01_0035.summary: The fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas are located at the west end of the Reactor Building and include spaces for the spent fuel pool, refueling pool, and dry dock, all designed to facilitate safe fuel handling and reactor maintenance operations. These areas provide radiation protection for personnel, support the transport and storage of both new and spent fuel assemblies, and are equipped with cranes and access points for efficient fuel movement and inspection.

nuscale_ch01_0648.title: DSRS 15.0, Rev 0: Introduction - Transient and Accident AnalysesI.5 Plant Characteristics Considered in the Safety Evaluation
nuscale_ch01_0648.summary: Section I.5, "Plant Characteristics Considered in the Safety Evaluation," confirms that the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report fully conforms to the relevant regulatory criteria for this aspect. No exceptions or deviations are noted for this section, indicating that all required plant characteristics have been appropriately considered in the safety evaluation.

nuscale_ch01_0016.title: Principal Site Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0016.summary: This section provides a general overview of the NuScale plant design, outlining key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, and waste management. It explains that each Combined License (COL) applicant must reference the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) and supplement it with site-specific details, while distinguishing between certified design elements and conceptual design information (CDI) that are outside the certified scope. Security-related information is specially marked and withheld according to regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides an overview of the NuScale plant design, summarizing key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling, waste management, and auxiliary systems. It explains that each Combined License (COL) applicant must reference the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) and provide site-specific details where required, while distinguishing between certified design elements and conceptual design information (CDI). Security-related information is specially marked and withheld according to regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0650.title: DSRS 15.0, Rev 0: Introduction - Transient and Accident AnalysesI.7 Evaluation of Individual Initiating Events
nuscale_ch01_0650.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for transient and accident analyses, specifically regarding the evaluation of individual initiating events. It states that the evaluation of individual initiating events fully conforms to the applicable regulatory guidance, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch01_0001.title: CHAPTER 1 INTRODUCTION AND GENERAL DESCRIPTION OF THE PLANT
nuscale_ch01_0001.summary: This section introduces the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), which is required for standard design certification under U.S. regulations. It provides an overview of the NuScale Power Module (NPM)—a modular, scalable nuclear steam supply system—and summarizes key design features such as passive safety (no power required for shutdown), compact containment, and advanced digital controls, applicable to facilities with up to twelve modules.

nuscale_ch01_0334.title: SRP 9.2.6, Rev 3: Condensate Storage FacilitiesII.2 Environmental and Dynamic Effects Design Basis
nuscale_ch01_0334.summary: The NuScale condensate storage system is not safety-related or risk-significant and is located outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, so its failure does not impact safety-related systems or plant operation. The system does not require design for safe-shutdown earthquake conditions, and sharing of the facility does not impair safety functions. There are no environmental, dynamic, or radioactive release concerns associated with the condensate storage system, and it complies with relevant regulatory guidance.

nuscale_ch01_0658.title: SRP 15.0.2, (March 2007): Review of Transient and Accident Analysis MethodsII.3 Code Assessment Departure
nuscale_ch01_0658.summary: This section explains that the NuScale design partially conforms to certain regulatory criteria for transient and accident analysis, with deviations primarily due to design differences from traditional BWRs and the use of a modified AST methodology for radiological consequences. NuScale’s evaluation models for loss-of-coolant accidents (LOCAs) address only the technically relevant features required by regulation, supporting exemptions from some portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K. The accident scenario identification process fully conforms to regulatory guidance.

nuscale_ch01_0002.title: Introduction
nuscale_ch01_0002.summary: This section introduces the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), which is required for standard design certification under U.S. regulations. It outlines the scope of the report, describing the NuScale Power Module (NPM) as a modular, scalable nuclear steam supply system, and highlights key safety and design features such as passive safety systems, compact containment, and advanced digital controls.

nuscale_ch01_0657.title: SRP 15.0.2, (March 2007): Review of Transient and Accident Analysis MethodsII.2 Accident Scenario Identification Process
nuscale_ch01_0657.summary: The section outlines NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for accident scenario identification, specifically referencing SRP 15.0.2 (March 2007). It states that NuScale fully conforms to the accident scenario identification process requirements, with no departures or exceptions noted for this aspect.

nuscale_ch01_0322.title: SRP 9.2.2, Rev 4: Reactor Auxiliary Cooling Water SystemII.2 Environmental and Dynamic Effects
nuscale_ch01_0322.summary: The section addresses the NuScale reactor's conformance with regulatory criteria for the Reactor Auxiliary Cooling Water System, specifically regarding environmental and dynamic effects. It states that the system partially conforms to the relevant SRP 9.2.2, Rev 4 requirements, with additional details provided in other referenced sections. The system is not classified as safety-related, so certain criteria are not applicable.

edges.description: Section A describes the design and safety features of the containment vessel for the NuScale modules, while Section B discusses the process of reviewing regulatory criteria and guidance to ensure the design (including containment) meets NRC requirements, thus connecting the design details in A to the regulatory conformance process in B.

Section A describes the design features of the containment vessel for the NuScale modules, which are relevant to Section B's discussion of how the plant's design (including containment) was reviewed for conformance with regulatory criteria in the Standard Review Plan.

Section B references specific sections that provide detailed material information for components described in Section A, connecting the general containment vessel design to the specific pressurizer materials.

Section A describes the overall containment and power output design for the 12-module plant, while Section B details the decay heat removal system (DHRS) analysis for a single module, with the relationship being that the single-module DHRS analysis in Section B is intended to demonstrate safety functions that, when applied to each module, collectively support the safety case for the 12-module plant described in Section A.

Section A (Generic Issues) references and builds upon the content of Section B (Conformance with Standard Review Plan) by addressing broader issues identified during the SRP gap analysis summarized in Section B.

Section A discusses the overall regulatory conformance review for the entire 12-module plant, while Section B provides detailed design information for a specific component (the pressurizer) relevant to single-module analysis, connecting them through the application of plant-wide regulatory criteria to individual module components.

Section A discusses the overall regulatory conformance review for the 12-module plant concept, while Section B details the design basis of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) for a single module, connecting them through the application of regulatory criteria from the multi-module plant to the analysis of a single module's safety system.

Section A discusses the overall regulatory conformance review for the multi-module (12-module) plant, while Section B provides specific design details for the containment vessel used in each module, connecting the general plant concept to the single-module design analyzed in the DHRS context.

Section A discusses the overall conformance of the 12-module plant design with regulatory criteria, while Section B provides detailed material specifications for pressurizer components relevant to a single module, connecting them through the application of plant-wide regulatory requirements to individual module analyses.

Section A describes the overall plant concept and regulatory conformance for the 12-module design, while Section B details the design basis and function of the DHRS for a single module, with the single-module DHRS analysis in Section B serving as a representative case to demonstrate compliance for the multi-module plant described in Section A.

Section A discusses the overall conformance of the 12-module plant design with regulatory criteria, while Section B provides specific design details of the containment vessel used in each module, connecting them through the application of regulatory standards to both the multi-module plant and the single-module analysis.

The 12-module plant concept in Ch.01 provides the overall plant configuration, of which the single-module DHRS analysis in Ch.05 is a representative case, meaning the single-module analysis is used to demonstrate safety for each module within the multi-module plant.

Section A provides detailed descriptions of pressurizer components and their materials for a single reactor module, while Section B outlines the general containment vessel design for the multi-module plant, connecting them through the shared design and safety requirements that apply to both single-module and multi-module configurations.

Section A details the design and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) for a single NuScale Power Module (NPM), while Section B describes the containment vessel design for each module in the 12-module plant concept, connecting them through the shared application of single-module safety analyses (like DHRS) to each module within the multi-module plant configuration.

Section A discusses the regulatory conformance of the site cooling water system for the multi-module NuScale plant, while Section B describes the design and safety classification of the containment vessel for a single module, connecting them through the context that the plant's overall safety approach (multi-module) relies on the integrity and independence of each single-module's safety systems, such as the DHRS analyzed in Ch.05.

Section B describes a comprehensive review process of SRP sections, including SRP 9.2.1 discussed in Section A, indicating that the conformance and applicability analysis in Section A is part of the broader gap analysis methodology outlined in Section B.

Section B describes a comprehensive review process of SRP sections, including SRP 9.2.1 referenced in Section A, establishing that the conformance analysis in Section A is part of the broader SRP applicability and gap analysis discussed in Section B.

Section A describes the technical containment design for the NuScale plant, while Section B discusses the plant's conformance with regulatory criteria, connecting them through the need for the containment design (A) to satisfy the regulatory requirements reviewed in B.

Section A describes the technical containment design of the NuScale module, while Section B discusses the review of regulatory criteria (SRP) that the design, including containment, must conform to, connecting them through the requirement that the containment design must satisfy regulatory acceptance criteria.

Section A provides a general description of the plant's design basis and operational limits for the 12-module concept, while Section B specifies the containment vessel design details that apply to each individual module, connecting the overall plant concept to the single-module analysis.

Section A provides a general description of the 12-module plant concept, which establishes the overall design context within which the single-module DHRS analysis in Section B (and Ch.05) is evaluated for regulatory conformance.

Section A provides a general description of the plant's design and operational limits, which sets the context for the regulatory conformance analysis detailed in Section B, where the plant's features (such as the 12-module concept) are evaluated against Standard Review Plan criteria.

Section A describes the system test program and methodology used to validate the safety analysis for a single NuScale Power Module (NPM), which provides the technical basis for the design and safety evaluation of the 12-module plant concept referenced in Section B.

Section A describes the test program that generates data for validating the NuScale module's safety analysis, which supports Section B's discussion of how the design conforms to regulatory criteria by providing evidence for meeting acceptance criteria.

Section A describes the generation of thermal-hydraulic data and safety methodology development for the NuScale plant (including the 12-module concept) using integral system tests, which provides technical support and validation for the regulatory conformance analysis discussed in Section B.

Section A discusses the protection of safety-related structures from failures in nonsafety-related systems like the SCWS, while Section B describes the containment vessel's design and classification, both of which are connected through the requirement that containment integrity (Section B) must not be compromised by failures in supporting systems (Section A) per regulatory criteria.

Section B provides the general description and design basis for the NuScale plant, which includes the 12-module concept, while Section A specifies how the plant's systems, such as the SCWS, conform to regulatory criteria based on that overall design.

Section B describes the integral system test program that generates data and validates safety codes for the single-module design analyzed in Ch.05, which provides supporting evidence for the multi-module plant concept described in Ch.01 (Section A) by ensuring that the safety-related phenomena are understood at the module level before scaling up.
Section A provides specific details about the containment vessel design for each module, which supports the general plant description and safety analysis framework outlined in Section B.

Section A discusses regulatory conformance aspects of DC power systems and station blackout independence for the NuScale plant, which are design and safety requirements that underpin the multi-module (12-module) plant concept described in Section B, where the containment and power output features are specified for each module.

Section A details specific conformance with regulatory guidance for DC power systems and station blackout independence, while Section B summarizes the overall approach and results of the plant's conformance review with the Standard Review Plan, thus Section A provides detailed evidence that supports the broader claims made in Section B.

Section A describes operational and maintenance features relevant to the 12-module plant layout, while Section B details the containment vessel design for a single module, connecting them by showing how single-module safety and maintenance considerations scale or integrate into the multi-module plant concept.

Section A outlines the plant characteristics and event categorization used in safety evaluations for the NuScale 12-module plant, while Section B discusses the conformance of these evaluations with regulatory guidance, indicating that the single-module DHRS analysis in Ch.05 is justified as representative for the multi-module plant based on regulatory acceptance criteria.

Section A outlines the plant characteristics and event categorization considered in the safety evaluation for the 12-module plant, while Section B describes the conformance review process with the Standard Review Plan, connecting them through the application of regulatory criteria and acceptance standards to both the multi-module plant concept and the single-module DHRS analysis.

Section A specifies detailed regulatory conformance for electrical and power systems that are part of the broader general plant description summarized in Section B, connecting specific design compliance to the overall facility description.

Both Section A and Section B discuss non-safety-related water systems (condensate storage and auxiliary cooling water) in the NuScale plant, indicating that failures or unavailability of these systems do not impact safety-related functions or accident mitigation, thus supporting the safety case for both single-module and multi-module (12-module) configurations.

Section A provides a general description of the 12-module plant design, which establishes the context and design basis for the single-module DHRS analysis detailed in Section B.

Section A discusses the accident analysis methods and regulatory conformance for the NuScale plant, including the evaluation of critical heat flux relevant to the multi-module (12-module) design, while Section B addresses the cooling water systems and their regulatory applicability, highlighting that the site cooling water system is not safety-related for accident mitigation, thus both sections relate through their treatment of safety analysis and system functions in the context of regulatory compliance for the NuScale design.
